title: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman documents Abby Johnson's Facebook opposition to The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act, in which she argued that abolishing abortion would cost Texas federal money and dismissed abolitionists as 'ignorant.' The article also rebuts her objection to legally penalizing parents who pay for abortions, citing Roe v. Wade's footnote 54 and testimony from former abortion-accomplice Jason Storms.
audiences: Pro-life advocates skeptical of immediate abolition

questions.question: What specific reason did Abby Johnson give for opposing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates skeptical of immediate abolition
questions.answer: Johnson opposed the bill on financial grounds, arguing that abolishing abortion in Texas would cost the state federal money and programs, while labeling abolitionists 'ignorant' and accusing them of having 'no legislative backbone.'
questions.quote: Like many other pro-life leaders, Johnson opposed The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act. Responding to an abolitionist on Facebook, she criticized the bill on the grounds that abolishing abortion would cost Texas federal money and programs. She then calls supporters of abolishing abortion in Texas “ignorant” and supporters of a strategy with “no legislative backbone.”

questions.question: Why does Silberman say Johnson's federal-funding objection is also economically illiterate?
questions.answer: Because Texas is a net donor state to the federal government—getting back only 57 cents per dollar sent to Washington—so the framing that federal money is a generous subsidy worth preserving over abolition collapses on its own terms.
questions.quote: Leaving aside that Johnson herself appears to be ignorant of the financial relationship between Texas and Washington D.C. (according to [Wallet Hub via *Dallas News*](https://www.dallasnews.com/business/economic-snapshot/2017/04/17/many-happy-returns-taxes-texas), Texas gets only $0.57 back for every dollar it sends to the federal government), what she’s saying here goes far beyond economic illiteracy.

questions.question: What pattern of behavior does the article allege about Johnson's response to abolitionist criticism?
questions.answer: Silberman reports that Johnson deleted the original comment thread and blocked the commenter, and that he has personally spoken with sixteen other people who say Johnson has blocked them for criticizing her opposition to abolition bills.
questions.quote: I would provide a link to the whole comment thread but, Johnson deleted the comments and blocked the commenter, Susan Burklund, according to Burklund. This appears to be a pattern as I’ve spoken directly with 16 other people who say Johnson has blocked them for criticizing her for opposing abolition bills.

questions.question: Why does the article argue that exempting parents from legal penalty effectively keeps abortion legal?
questions.answer: If only the abortionist faces prosecution while parents have automatic immunity, then do-it-yourself abortion remains legal—and since the industry is already shifting toward DIY methods, such laws merely accelerate that evolution rather than ending abortion.
questions.quote: First, abortion isn’t actually illegal if parents who murder their sons and daughters have automatic immunity. If only the abortionist faces justice, do-it-yourself abortion is legal. And with the abortion industry moving toward DIY abortion anyway, a bill providing legal penalty only for the abortionist accomplishes nothing but expedite the evolution of the abortion industry.

questions.question: How does Jason Storms's testimony illustrate the deterrent function of criminalizing parents?
questions.answer: Storms told the Texas committee that he and his high school girlfriend would never have aborted their child had they known they would face homicide charges, arguing that consistent equal-protection law would prevent the killings rather than merely punish them.
questions.quote: “Here’s a fact: my girlfriend and I, if we knew we would’ve been facing homicide charges, would never have aborted that child. That child would be alive today. I’d have a 22-year-old little child that I could celebrate life with right now that’s not here. The law is a deterrent to crime. We shouldn’t think of this only as a matter of putting a woman on the stand. We should think of this as a great deterrent. Men and women would not think of doing this if we stood firm on the law and provided equal protection for these children.”

questions.question: How does the article connect parental immunity in pre-Roe abortion statutes to the legalization of abortion itself?
questions.answer: Justice Harry Blackmun's footnote 54 in Roe v. Wade pointed to Texas's inconsistency—not treating the woman as a principal or accomplice and assigning lesser penalties than for murder—as evidence that even pro-life lawmakers did not really regard the preborn as persons, providing the legal opening for legalization.
questions.quote: “There are other inconsistencies between Fourteenth Amendment status and the typical abortion statute. It has already been pointed out, n. 49, supra, that in Texas the woman is not a principal or an accomplice with respect to an abortion upon her. If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?”

questions.question: How does the article respond to the claim that hard circumstances should give mothers automatic legal immunity?
questions.answer: It analogizes to other crimes—armed robbery driven by hunger, violence rooted in past abuse, drunk driving after personal trauma—where mitigating circumstances may justify lenient sentences but never blanket immunity, and notes that abolition bills leave specific charges to prosecutors, judges, and juries.
questions.quote: An armed robber might go hungry without the $150 in the till at the corner store. A man might be driven to acts of violence by the physical abuse he suffered as a child. A drunk driver who kills someone might have been drinking because her husband just left her. Such circumstances may warrant a more lenient sentence in some cases, but they do not remove the need for justice to be served.

questions.question: What is the practical body count Silberman attributes to killing the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act?
questions.answer: Because the Texas legislature meets only every two years, the bill's defeat is described as a literal death sentence for 110,000 preborn human beings who will be killed and discarded before another opportunity arises.
questions.quote: The Texas legislature meets only every two years, meaning the killing of the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act is a literal and undeniable death sentence for 110,000 preborn human beings. They will die and be cast into landfills, incineration chambers, and down garbage disposals so that Jeff Leach and his allies among pro-life lobbyists and state Republican leaders can virtue signal that they’re not like those mean abolitionists who actually think preborn human beings should get equal justice and protection.

questions.question: Does the article note any later shift in Abby Johnson's position?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocates skeptical of immediate abolition
questions.answer: Yes—an editor's note prepended to the piece reports that Johnson has since amended her position on this subject, and links to a video where she states her updated view.
questions.quote: *\*We are very happy to report that Abby Johnson has since amended her position on this subject. [See this video for her updated position.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bBmMZvGn6Cw)*

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: The article quotes this earlier piece extensively for its arguments on parental criminalization, deterrence, and Roe footnote 54.

related.title: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
related.reason: Provides the legislative backdrop for the Texas abolition bill that Johnson opposed and frames the 110,000-lives stake referenced here.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Continues Silberman's broader critique of pro-life institutional opposition to Texas abolition legislation.

related.title: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
related.reason: Names Abby Johnson among pro-life figures Free the States identifies as obstacles to state-level abolition strategy.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Lays out the equal-protection / criminalization framework that Johnson's exemption-for-parents stance directly rejects.

related.title: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
related.reason: A post-abortive mother's first-person case for criminalization, complementing Storms's deterrent testimony cited in this article.

related.title: "I oppose abortion, but there are procedures bills have to follow"
related.reason: Addresses the same genre of pro-life proceduralist deflections that Johnson deploys to oppose abolition bills.
